Yeah, I think there's two skills that are going to be the most critical skills for any knowledge worker, but especially for anyone working at GrowthX.
For one, at First Principles, number two is delegation.
And so First Principles, it simply just means how are you approaching every single task, every problem, every job to be done?
How are you decomposing that?
And how do you break it down to the atoms, if you will, right?
So if we say, hey, let's come up with a public.
What's your first thought?
And the goal here is to get us to thinking first principles, which is, okay, let's think about this company first.
What is this company?
What is their story?
What is the context?
How do they get created?
What do they do?
Who do they do it for?
Why do people love that company?
You know, like understand deeply, just like understanding a person, right?
You don't just get in a relationship with the person, you get to know the person first, right?
And when you do that, you have to ask good questions.
You have to think deeply what motivates this person, what are their fears, what are their motivations, what are their drivers, right?
And asking good questions is super critical here.
Then from that, now you're seeking a deeper understanding of the company.
And from there, it's like, okay, now let's go and seek a deeper understanding of the audience that we're trying.
Serve, right?
And when you think like a publisher, not like a marketer, it's not an audience you're trying to sell to, it's an audience you're trying to build trust with, right?
And so what that means is, it's very simple, is how do you build empathy for that audience, but how do you almost start to build a profile of that audience as if it was one single person?
So that's something I do a lot, right?
It's like, okay, let's actually use AI to start to describe this person as an individual human, you know, and build a real profile.
What's their day-to-day like?
What are their fears?
What are their questions they have?
What are the reasons they will get promoted or fired?
What are the, you know, the externalities that they need to think about, right?

And then once you build all of that, now you have like all the kind of building blocks to just start to think through and connect some of those dots to build ideally this publication or suggest the publication.
For this brand and this company, right?
And then the third part here along those lines is everyone here should be reading an obscene amount.
You should be saving things that you like.
You should be creating libraries of good written sentences or good writing or authors or, you know, in different spaces.

And, and so then what I usually do and I would recommend is like, okay, now let's go find publications in their space or not in that space that you think will fit this archetype of their brand, if you will.
Right.
And then when you combine all of this together, then the idea is like, okay, now let's come up with an angle, almost like kind of the editorial being a campaign, right?
It's like, hey, let's come up with a name for this publication.
Let's come up with an angle.
How will this publication serve this audience?
What is the gap in the market?
Not the gap in the product, but what is the gap in knowledge that is being served to that audience, right?

So if you do some research in the space and you do some searches and it comes across as a bunch of vendor blogs that are all garbage that don't really serve a good purpose, you're like, okay, the gap here is there's no really unique publication or there's a lot of noise and no one is kind of curating the right information, whatever that might be.
Like find the unique angle there, right?
So that's all like kind of thinking of first principles.
And then the other part that I mentioned is delegation, right?
If you, I have a two-year-old, but for any of you that have kids or, you know, family members with kids, they're young, right?
Like how do you explain something to a child, right?
How do you explain something to someone that has zero context in order for them to do a good job?
You have to give them...
The context, you have to explain the steps, you have to essentially be able to delegate, again, like from first principles.
And so think of a lot of what we're doing here as like building the muscles to be able to delegate to AI.
But you can't delegate to AI something that if you try to delegate the same way to a human that with no context, that human would not do a good job.
And so this is what we see, things fall flat, right?
So what we see is often we have automation, we have workflows, we have AI, but that doesn't substitute for lazy thinking.
And what I mean by that is like you still need to think, you still need to connect the dots, you still need to explain things, you still need to communicate with clarity, with preciseness, right?
And you need to be able to articulate yourself in a way that another human would understand.
And so this assignment, I think.
Connects those two things and hopefully gets us to kind of start practicing those muscles really well, but also having some fun and showing creativity.
At the end of the day, like our customers should be excited about what we're producing.
It should be better than what they can produce by themselves.
And if it's not, then we're not doing something big.

It's like super clear in my head, the thought process, right?
I am personally, I'm a frameworks and mental model person.
